title: Learning PyTorch from Scratch
text:
Preface
This book is for readers who know a little PyTorch, but not in a systematic way, and still do not yet feel ready to build things on their own.
The goal is not to cover every API. It is to help you build a learning path that actually leads somewhere:

First, understand tensors, autograd, and the basic mechanics of training models.
Then, learn the practical skills you need for real work: data loading, training loops, validation, and saving models.
Finally, use small but useful projects to move from “I can read the code” to “I can change it and write my own.”

This book tries to do three things well:

Stay clear: each chapter focuses on a small number of core ideas instead of dumping concepts.
Stay runnable: the Python examples in examples/ and code/ are meant to be used as starting points.
Stay practical: the examples are chosen to feel closer to real training tasks, not just toy formulas.

You do not need to understand every line on the first pass. A better way to study is:

Understand the main idea of a chapter.
Run the matching code.
Change a few pieces and see what happens.
Come back and fill in the details.

If you keep working that way, this book can gradually become your own PyTorch learning lab.
crumbs: Preface

title: 1  How to Use This Book
text:
This is not a dictionary-style PyTorch reference. It is a learning path that starts with core code patterns and ends with applying them to your own tasks.
I strongly recommend reading the first six chapters in order, because they build the foundation for everything that follows:

Chapter 1: build a mental map of the codebase and the core pieces of PyTorch.
Chapter 2: learn tensors, shapes, and autograd.
Chapter 3: understand the standard training loop, the most important template in deep learning code.
Chapter 4: understand Dataset and DataLoader, which answer the question “how does data actually get into the model?”
Chapter 5: complete a full classification task with a multilayer perceptron.
Chapter 6: move into convolutional networks and image classification.

The later chapters are more practical:

Transfer learning: how to use pretrained models when you do not have much data.
Debugging and acceleration: what to do when training is slow, loss does not fall, or memory runs out.
Small projects: combine earlier pieces into tasks of your own.

As you study, keep coming back to these four questions:

What is the shape of the input tensor?
What is the shape of the model output?
What exactly is the loss function comparing?
At what point do backpropagation and parameter updates happen?

As those four questions become clearer, you stop memorizing code and start understanding what training is actually doing.
The final skill this book is trying to give you is very specific:
not just the ability to copy a tutorial, but the ability to decide for yourself:

Is this a classification task, a regression task, or something else?
How should I organize the data?
What should the first version of the model be?
Which loss function and training loop fit this problem?

In other words, the goal is not to “remember PyTorch.” The goal is to translate your own problems into PyTorch.
crumbs: 1  How to Use This Book

title: 2  Chapter 1 A Map of the Code
text:
2.1 The Study Order
In this book, we do not begin with abstract concepts. We begin with code.
What you really need is not a list of PyTorch terms. You need to be able to open a training script and understand what each part is doing.
The book is built around the code/ directory. I recommend reading and running the files in this order:
That order is intentional.
It is not organized like a glossary. It follows the order in which these ideas actually appear when you build a project from the ground up.
crumbs: 2  Chapter 1 A Map of the Code

title: 2  Chapter 1 A Map of the Code
text:
code/01_tensor_basics.py
code/02_autograd_basics.py
code/03_linear_regression.py
code/04_custom_dataset.py
code/05_mlp_classifier.py
code/06_cnn_classifier.py
code/07_transfer_learning.py
crumbs: 2  Chapter 1 A Map of the Code

title: 2  Chapter 1 A Map of the Code
section: 2.2 What Each File Teaches
text:
2.2 What Each File Teaches

2.2.1 01_tensor_basics.py
This file answers the most basic question: what does data look like inside PyTorch?
Here you should understand:

what a tensor is
what shape means
what reshape, unsqueeze, and flatten do



2.2.2 02_autograd_basics.py
Then we move into gradients.
Here you should understand:

which tensors need gradients
what loss.backward() actually does
why parameters have a .grad



2.2.3 03_linear_regression.py
This is the first full training loop.
It matters because the later classification, CNN, and transfer learning examples all reuse the same basic structure. The models and data get more complex, but the training template stays recognizable.


2.2.4 04_custom_dataset.py
This is where we start dealing with a real practical question: how does data get batched and fed into the model?


2.2.5 05_mlp_classifier.py
This is the first real end-to-end task, where earlier pieces come together in a complete classification project.


2.2.6 06_cnn_classifier.py
This upgrades the model from a fully connected network to a convolutional one, so we can work with image structure more naturally.


2.2.7 07_transfer_learning.py
This moves the book from teaching examples toward techniques that are common in real projects.
crumbs: 2  Chapter 1 A Map of the Code

title: 2  Chapter 1 A Map of the Code
section: 2.3 The Main Goal of This Chapter
text:
2.3 The Main Goal of This Chapter
The point is not to memorize file names. The point is to develop a reliable way to read code:

First look at the inputs and outputs.
Then look at how the model is defined.
Then look at how the loss is computed.
Then look at how parameters are updated.
Finally, look at validation and debugging.

That reading order will keep helping you long after this book.
crumbs: 2  Chapter 1 A Map of the Code

title: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code
text:
3.1 Read code/01_tensor_basics.py Line by Line
This chapter revolves around just two files:
Run both of them before reading too deeply. The ideas are much easier to understand once you have seen the code in action.
This file is short, which makes it a good place to practice reading PyTorch carefully.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code

title: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code
text:
3.1.1 Step 1: Create a 1D tensor
x = torch.arange(12, dtype=torch.float32)
inspect_tensor("x", x)
There are two things to notice here:

torch.arange(12) creates 12 numbers, from 0 through 11.
dtype=torch.float32 stores them as floating-point values.

At this point, the shape of x is [12].
Do not rush past that. Start building the habit of asking:
what is the dimensionality of this tensor, and what is its shape right now?


3.1.2 Step 2: Turn a 1D tensor into a matrix
matrix = x.reshape(3, 4)
inspect_tensor("matrix", matrix)
This step does not change the data values. It changes how the data is organized.
In other words:

the values are still the same 12 values
but the view changes from one long vector into a 3 x 4 matrix

That is one of the most important ideas in early PyTorch learning:
many operations change shape rather than changing the values themselves.


3.1.3 Step 3: Add a batch dimension by hand
batch = matrix.unsqueeze(0)
inspect_tensor("batch", batch)
unsqueeze(0) inserts a new dimension of length 1 at dimension 0.
So the shape changes from:

[3, 4]

to:

[1, 3, 4]

Why does this matter?
Because real models usually process batches of samples, not just one sample at a time.
That extra first dimension is often the batch dimension.


3.1.4 Step 4: Flatten each sample again
flattened = batch.flatten(start_dim=1)
inspect_tensor("flattened", flattened)
Here start_dim=1 means:

keep dimension 0 unchanged
flatten everything from dimension 1 onward

So the shape changes from [1, 3, 4] to [1, 12].
This is the same basic idea you will later see in Flatten() layers inside MLP models.
Even though this example is tiny, you are already looking at one of the most common shape transformations in neural network code.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code

title: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code
section: 3.2 What Should You Watch for in Tensor Code?
text:
3.2 What Should You Watch for in Tensor Code?
Whenever you read tensor code, focus on these three things first:

What is the current shape?
What will the next shape be?
Why is the shape changing?

If you always focus on individual values, model code will feel much harder than it needs to.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code

title: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code
section: 3.3 Why Shape Usually Matters More Than Values
text:
3.3 Why Shape Usually Matters More Than Values
At the beginning it is easy to stare at values like 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0.
But in deep learning code, shapes are usually more important:

[12]
[3, 4]
[1, 3, 4]
[1, 12]

Many bugs are not numerical mistakes. They are shape mistakes.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code

title: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code
section: 3.4 Now Read code/02_autograd_basics.py Line by Line
text:
3.4 Now Read code/02_autograd_basics.py Line by Line
This file introduces trainable parameters for the first time:
weight = torch.tensor(2.0, requires_grad=True)
bias = torch.tensor(1.0, requires_grad=True)
The key point here is not the syntax. It is the role of each tensor:

the input x does not need to be learned
the parameters weight and bias do need to be learned

That is why only the parameters use requires_grad=True.

3.4.1 Step 1: Compute the prediction
y_pred = weight * x + bias
This is the simplest possible linear model.
It says:

take each input x
multiply by the weight weight
add the bias bias
get the prediction y_pred



3.4.2 Step 2: Compute the loss
loss = ((y_pred - y_true) ** 2).mean()
This answers a very practical question:
how far are the model’s predictions from the true answers right now?
Here we use mean squared error.


3.4.3 Step 3: Run backpropagation
In this code:
loss.backward()
you can think of the process like this:

use the current parameters to compute predictions
compare those predictions with the true targets
ask PyTorch to compute how the parameters should move

That third step is backpropagation.
Afterward, weight.grad and bias.grad contain values.
Those gradients are not the new parameters. They are signals that tell us how the parameters should be updated.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code

title: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code
section: 3.5 Put the Two Files Together
text:
3.5 Put the Two Files Together
01_tensor_basics.py teaches you how data is shaped.
02_autograd_basics.py teaches you how parameters receive gradients through the loss.
Together they form the foundation of later training code:

data must be organized in the right shapes
parameters must be part of the computation so gradients can flow to them
crumbs: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code

title: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code
section: 3.6 The Big Ideas Behind the Code
text:
3.6 The Big Ideas Behind the Code
After these two files, you should be able to summarize the core ideas:

Tensors are PyTorch’s basic data structure.
In deep learning code, shape is often the first thing to check.
Parameters differ from normal inputs because parameters need to be learned.
backward() computes gradients. It does not update parameters.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code

title: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code
section: 3.7 What You Should Change Yourself
text:
3.7 What You Should Change Yourself
Try editing a few things and rerun the code:

In 01_tensor_basics.py, change reshape(3, 4) to another valid shape.
In 02_autograd_basics.py, change the initial values of weight and bias.
Observe how loss and .grad change.

That is how PyTorch starts to feel natural: not by memorizing APIs, but by watching shapes and gradients behave.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 2 From Tensor Code to Gradient Code

title: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression
text:
4.1 First, Look at the Structure of the Code
This chapter focuses on a single file: code/03_linear_regression.py.
It may look simple, but it is one of the most important files in the book.
Why? Because almost every more advanced task later on still relies on the same training structure.
This file has three main parts:
That is the basic shape of a minimal but usable training project.
crumbs: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression

title: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression
text:
make_dataset(): creates training data
train_one_epoch(): wraps one epoch of training
main(): builds the model, loss, and optimizer, then runs training
crumbs: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression

title: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression
section: 4.2 Start with make_dataset()
text:
4.2 Start with make_dataset()
features = torch.randn(num_samples, 1)
noise = 0.2 * torch.randn(num_samples, 1)
targets = 3.5 * features + 1.2 + noise
return TensorDataset(features, targets)
This code creates a dataset where there really is a pattern between input and target.
You can read it like this:

features are the inputs
targets are the true answers
the underlying rule is roughly y = 3.5x + 1.2
some noise is added to make it more realistic

The point of this setup is that we do not have to think about messy real-world data yet. We can focus on the training process itself.
crumbs: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression

title: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression
section: 4.3 Then Look at the Setup in main()
text:
4.3 Then Look at the Setup in main()
dataset = make_dataset()
loader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=True)

model = nn.Linear(1, 1)
criterion = nn.MSELoss()
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.1)
These lines assemble the four essential pieces of a training task:

data
model
loss function
optimizer

You will do this again and again in your own projects.
crumbs: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression

title: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression
section: 4.4 Why Linear Regression Is the Right Starting Point
text:
4.4 Why Linear Regression Is the Right Starting Point
It works so well for learning because:

the data is simple
the model is simple
the loss is simple
the training behavior is easy to interpret

That lets you focus on the loop itself instead of being distracted by model complexity.
crumbs: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression

title: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression
section: 4.5 Now Read train_one_epoch() Line by Line
text:
4.5 Now Read train_one_epoch() Line by Line
This is the part you should keep coming back to:
for inputs, targets in loader:
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    predictions = model(inputs)
    loss = criterion(predictions, targets)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
Whenever you read a PyTorch training script, your eyes should learn to find these steps almost immediately.

4.5.1 for inputs, targets in loader:
Training does not happen on the whole dataset at once. It happens one batch at a time.
Why?

it uses memory more efficiently
it trains faster
it works better with GPUs



4.5.2 optimizer.zero_grad()
This clears the old gradients.
If you forget this step, gradients accumulate, and training usually behaves incorrectly.


4.5.3 predictions = model(inputs)
This is the forward pass.
The model uses its current parameters to make predictions for this batch.


4.5.4 loss = criterion(predictions, targets)
This compares predictions to targets and produces a scalar loss.
You can think of it as “how wrong is the model on this batch right now?”


4.5.5 loss.backward()
This computes gradients for the model parameters.


4.5.6 optimizer.step()
This is the step that actually updates the parameters.
Beginners often blur backward() and step() together. Keep the distinction clear:

backward() computes gradients
step() uses those gradients to change parameters
crumbs: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression

title: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression
section: 4.6 The Training Template Hidden in the Code
text:
4.6 The Training Template Hidden in the Code
This loop is really just six actions:

get a batch
clear old gradients
run the forward pass
compute the loss
run backpropagation
update the parameters

If you can see those six actions clearly, more advanced code becomes much easier to read.
crumbs: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression

title: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression
section: 4.7 Finally, Look at the Epoch Loop
text:
4.7 Finally, Look at the Epoch Loop
for epoch in range(1, 21):
    avg_loss = train_one_epoch(model, loader, criterion, optimizer)
    print(f"epoch={epoch:02d} loss={avg_loss:.4f}")
An epoch means one full pass through the training set.
At this point, two ideas should become clear:

a batch is the basic unit of training work
multiple epochs are needed for the model to improve gradually
crumbs: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression

title: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression
section: 4.8 What You Are Really Learning Here
text:
4.8 What You Are Really Learning Here
This chapter is not really about linear regression.
It is about learning to see almost any training script as a variation on the same core pattern.
Once that pattern becomes familiar, CNNs, transfer learning, and even much larger models start to feel less mysterious.
crumbs: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression

title: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression
section: 4.9 Three Experiments to Try Right Away
text:
4.9 Three Experiments to Try Right Away

Change the learning rate from 0.1 to 1.0 and see whether training becomes unstable.
Change the learning rate to 0.001 and see whether convergence becomes much slower.
Change the batch size and watch whether the loss becomes smoother or noisier.
crumbs: 4  Chapter 3 Learning the Training Loop Through Linear Regression

title: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset
text:
5.1 First, Look at the Structure
This chapter is built around code/04_custom_dataset.py.
In the previous chapter, we learned what the training loop looks like. Now we need to answer a key question:
where does the loader in that training loop actually come from?
This file does two things:
The example is tiny, but it captures the core logic of how data enters a real PyTorch project.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset

title: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset
text:
defines StudentScoreDataset
wraps it in a DataLoader
crumbs: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset

title: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset
section: 5.2 Start with StudentScoreDataset
text:
5.2 Start with StudentScoreDataset
class StudentScoreDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self.features = ...
        self.labels = ...
The basic idea is very simple:

features store the inputs
labels store the targets

This time we do not use a built-in dataset. We create a tiny tabular dataset ourselves.
That makes it easier to focus on how Dataset works.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset

title: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset
section: 5.3 What Is a Dataset, Really?
text:
5.3 What Is a Dataset, Really?
From the code’s point of view, a dataset only needs to answer two questions:

How many samples are there?
If I give you an index, what sample do you return?

That is why a custom dataset implements:

__len__
__getitem__

This is not just syntax. It is the core design.
PyTorch does not care whether your data comes from a spreadsheet, an image folder, or a database. It only needs a consistent way to request one sample at a time.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset

title: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset
section: 5.4 Understand __len__ and __getitem__
crumbs: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset

title: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset
section: 5.5 Then What Does DataLoader Do?
text:
5.5 Then What Does DataLoader Do?
DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=2, shuffle=True) says:

do not feed one sample at a time
group samples into batches
shuffle them during training

That is why the later training loop can be written naturally as:
for inputs, labels in loader:
    ...
crumbs: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset

title: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset
section: 5.6 Finally, Look at What the Main Program Prints
text:
5.6 Finally, Look at What the Main Program Prints
for batch_idx, (inputs, labels) in enumerate(loader, start=1):
    print(f"batch {batch_idx}")
    print("inputs shape:", tuple(inputs.shape))
    print("labels shape:", tuple(labels.shape))
This is a habit worth keeping.
Do not guess what a batch looks like. Print it and inspect it.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset

title: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset
section: 5.7 The Most Important Habit from This Chapter
text:
5.7 The Most Important Habit from This Chapter
When you start building your own project, do not begin with the model.
Ask yourself first:

What does one sample look like?
What does one label look like?
What shape will they have after batching?

If those three things are unclear, model training usually breaks very quickly.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset

title: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset
section: 5.8 The Main Ideas Behind the File
text:
5.8 The Main Ideas Behind the File
After reading this chapter, you should understand:

Dataset defines individual samples.
DataLoader organizes batched iteration.
shuffle=True is usually used for training data, not validation data.
Label shape and dtype must match the loss function you plan to use.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset

title: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset
section: 5.9 Exercises to Try Immediately
text:
5.9 Exercises to Try Immediately

Add more samples to StudentScoreDataset.
Change batch_size=2 to 3 or 4.
Observe how the batch shapes change.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 4 Understanding Data Flow Through a Custom Dataset

title: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification
text:
6.1 Break the Code into Modules First
This chapter introduces the first file that really feels like a complete project: code/05_mlp_classifier.py.
If the earlier chapters were about gathering building blocks, this chapter is where those blocks begin to feel like a working system.
This file has four main parts:
That is already very close to the structure of a real training script.
crumbs: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification

title: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification
text:
MLPClassifier: defines the model
evaluate(): defines validation logic
the dataset and DataLoader
the training loop in main()
crumbs: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification

title: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification
section: 6.2 Start by Reading the Model Class
text:
6.2 Start by Reading the Model Class
class MLPClassifier(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        self.network = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Flatten(),
            nn.Linear(28 * 28, 128),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(128, 10),
        )
The main idea to build here is simple:
defining a model means defining how the input data moves through layers and what kind of output comes out at the end.

6.2.1 nn.Flatten()
MNIST images are 2D images, but a Linear layer expects a 1D vector for each sample.
So the first step is to flatten the image.


6.2.2 nn.Linear(28 * 28, 128)
This says:

each image starts as 784 numbers
the hidden representation will have 128 features



6.2.3 nn.ReLU()
This adds nonlinearity, so the model is not just stacking linear operations.


6.2.4 nn.Linear(128, 10)
This produces 10 class scores, one for each digit from 0 to 9.
crumbs: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification

title: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification
section: 6.3 Why This File Is Worth Revisiting
text:
6.3 Why This File Is Worth Revisiting
This is the first example that pulls everything together:

tensors
batching
a model
a loss function
an optimizer
validation accuracy

When you can read this file comfortably, you have started moving from “learning parts” to “understanding a full training script.”
crumbs: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification

title: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification
section: 6.4 Why Is forward() So Short?
crumbs: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification

title: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification
section: 6.5 Now Read the Data Section
text:
6.5 Now Read the Data Section
train_dataset = datasets.MNIST(...)
test_dataset = datasets.MNIST(...)

train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=128, shuffle=True)
test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=256, shuffle=False)
Two important things happen here:

the training and test sets are separated
each gets its own DataLoader

Pay attention to a common pattern:

training data usually uses shuffle=True
test or validation data usually uses shuffle=False
crumbs: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification

title: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification
section: 6.6 Follow the Shape of the Data Through the Model
text:
6.6 Follow the Shape of the Data Through the Model

The input image starts as [batch_size, 1, 28, 28]
After Flatten(), it becomes [batch_size, 784]
The hidden layer maps it into a learned feature space
The final output becomes [batch_size, 10]

Those 10 values are not probabilities yet. They are logits: raw class scores.
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
These two lines decide:

how the model is judged
how the parameters are updated
crumbs: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification

title: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification
section: 6.7 Why Use CrossEntropyLoss?
text:
6.7 Why Use CrossEntropyLoss?
Because this is a multiclass classification task.
It typically expects:

model outputs of shape [batch_size, num_classes]
labels of shape [batch_size]
labels stored as class indices, not one-hot vectors

That is why reading classification code always means checking both output shape and label format.
crumbs: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification

title: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification
section: 6.8 Read the Training Loop Carefully
text:
6.8 Read the Training Loop Carefully
for images, labels in train_loader:
    images = images.to(device)
    labels = labels.to(device)

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    logits = model(images)
    loss = criterion(logits, labels)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
By now, you should notice something important:
this has almost exactly the same structure as the training loop from Chapter 3.
The main things that changed are:

the data
the model
the loss function

That is one of the most important lessons in learning PyTorch for your own work.
You do not need to learn a brand-new system every time. You learn how to swap pieces into a familiar training skeleton.
crumbs: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification

title: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification
section: 6.9 evaluate() Matters Too
text:
6.9 evaluate() Matters Too
A lot of beginners learn how to train, but not how to validate.
In real projects, validation matters just as much.
The key pieces here are:

model.eval()
torch.no_grad()
argmax(dim=1)

They solve three different problems:

switch the model into evaluation mode
turn off gradient computation
convert class scores into predicted class labels
crumbs: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification

title: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification
section: 6.10 A Good Order for Reading This Kind of Code
text:
6.10 A Good Order for Reading This Kind of Code
Whenever you read a classification script, try this order:

What shape do the inputs have?
How many dimensions does the final output have?
Which loss function is used?
What format are the labels in?
How is accuracy computed?
crumbs: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification

title: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification
section: 6.11 Good Changes to Try Right Away
text:
6.11 Good Changes to Try Right Away

Change the hidden size from 128 to 256
Add another Linear + ReLU block
Change the optimizer from Adam to SGD

That is when you start to feel how model design, optimization, and training results affect one another in practice.
crumbs: 6  Chapter 5 Learning a Full Project Through MLP Classification

title: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code
text:
7.1 Start with the Overall Structure
This chapter centers on code/06_cnn_classifier.py.
If the previous chapter was your first complete classification project, this one is your first project that treats images like images.
The file still contains the same major pieces:
That is worth noticing.
It tells you something important:
when the task type stays the same, the broad structure of a PyTorch project often stays stable. The biggest changes usually happen inside the model.
crumbs: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code

title: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code
text:
the model class SimpleCNN
the validation function evaluate()
the dataset and DataLoader
the training loop in main()
crumbs: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code

title: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code
section: 7.2 Read SimpleCNN in Parts
text:
7.2 Read SimpleCNN in Parts
self.features = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Conv2d(1, 16, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.MaxPool2d(2),
    nn.Conv2d(16, 32, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.MaxPool2d(2),
)
This part is the feature extractor.
Try not to memorize terminology yet. Read it as a sequence of shape changes.

7.2.1 Layer 1: nn.Conv2d(1, 16, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
The most important parts here are:

the input channel count is 1
the output channel count is 16

For FashionMNIST, the input shape is usually [batch_size, 1, 28, 28].
After this convolution, the shape becomes:

[batch_size, 16, 28, 28]

Why do height and width stay 28 x 28?
Because padding=1 keeps the spatial size unchanged for this kernel size.


7.2.2 Layer 2: nn.MaxPool2d(2)
This layer does not change the number of channels, but it cuts the spatial size in half.
So the shape becomes:

[batch_size, 16, 14, 14]



7.2.3 The Next Convolution and Pooling Layers
After the second convolution and pooling steps, the shape becomes:

[batch_size, 32, 14, 14]
[batch_size, 32, 7, 7]

This is the habit worth building:
after every major layer, ask yourself what the shape is now.
crumbs: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code

title: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code
section: 7.3 Why Is the Classifier Written This Way?
text:
7.3 Why Is the Classifier Written This Way?
self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Flatten(),
    nn.Linear(32 * 7 * 7, 10),
)
This part turns extracted features into class scores.

7.3.1 nn.Flatten()
The convolutional block outputs a 4D tensor.
The fully connected layer expects a 2D tensor, so we flatten first.


7.3.2 nn.Linear(32 * 7 * 7, 10)
This is not an arbitrary number.
It comes directly from the shape calculation above:

channels: 32
height and width: 7 x 7

So each image becomes 32 * 7 * 7 features before classification.
If you get this number wrong, the model usually fails with a dimension mismatch.
crumbs: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code

title: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code
section: 7.4 What Does forward() Show You?
text:
7.4 What Does forward() Show You?
def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    x = self.features(x)
    return self.classifier(x)
These two lines are a clear example of how CNN code is often organized:

first extract features
then classify

You will see this pattern again in many more advanced vision models.
crumbs: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code

title: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code
section: 7.5 Notice What Did Not Change
text:
7.5 Notice What Did Not Change
for images, labels in train_loader:
    images = images.to(device)
    labels = labels.to(device)

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    logits = model(images)
    loss = criterion(logits, labels)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
This is almost the same training template as the previous chapter.
That is one of the most useful long-term lessons in PyTorch:
what transfers best is not memorizing one specific model, but learning which parts are stable and which parts can be swapped out.
crumbs: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code

title: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code
section: 7.6 The Main Ideas to Take Away
text:
7.6 The Main Ideas to Take Away

CNNs suit image tasks because they preserve local spatial structure.
Convolution layers often change the number of channels.
Pooling layers often reduce height and width.
The input size of a fully connected layer must come from shape reasoning, not guessing.
The model may change, but the training loop often stays largely the same.
crumbs: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code

title: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code
section: 7.7 Useful Changes to Try
text:
7.7 Useful Changes to Try

Change the first convolution from 16 output channels to 32
Add another convolution layer to features
Recompute the input size of the final Linear layer yourself
Temporarily print x.shape inside forward() and compare it with your manual reasoning
crumbs: 7  Chapter 6 Understanding Image Models Through CNN Code

title: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project
text:
8.1 What Is Different from Earlier Chapters?
This chapter is built around code/07_transfer_learning.py.
Its importance is simple: this is no longer just a teaching example. It is a starting point that many real projects would actually use.
In the earlier chapters, we built models from scratch.
This file starts with:
That means we are loading a model that has already learned from a large image dataset.
So the goal changes. We are no longer asking the model to learn everything from scratch. We are asking it to adapt existing visual knowledge to a new task.
crumbs: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project

title: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project
text:
weights = models.ResNet18_Weights.DEFAULT
model = models.resnet18(weights=weights)
crumbs: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project

title: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project
section: 8.2 Read build_model() Step by Step
text:
8.2 Read build_model() Step by Step

8.2.1 Step 1: Load a pretrained model
model = models.resnet18(weights=weights)
You can think of this model as no longer being a blank slate.
It has already learned many general visual features such as edges, textures, and local patterns.
That is what makes transfer learning useful.


8.2.2 Step 2: Freeze the existing parameters
for parameter in model.parameters():
    parameter.requires_grad = False
This means:
do not update the original feature extraction parameters yet.
Why do this?
Because if your dataset is small, training the whole large model immediately can be less stable and more data-hungry than you want.


8.2.3 Step 3: Replace the final classification head
model.fc = nn.Linear(model.fc.in_features, num_classes)
This is one of the most important lines in the whole file.
It says:

keep the earlier feature extractor
replace the final layer with a new one that matches your task

If your task has 3 classes, the output should have 3 dimensions. If it has 5 classes, the output should have 5.
crumbs: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project

title: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project
section: 8.3 What Is the Main Program Checking?
text:
8.3 What Is the Main Program Checking?
total_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
trainable_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad)
These lines are not just decorative logging.
They verify something important:
how many parameters the model has in total, and how many are still trainable after freezing.
That is a very practical debugging check in transfer learning work.
crumbs: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project

title: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project
section: 8.4 Why This Template Is So Useful for Your Own Work
text:
8.4 Why This Template Is So Useful for Your Own Work
This setup fits a very common real-world situation:

you have your own image classification task
your dataset is not huge
you want a strong first version quickly

In that case, transfer learning is often a better starting point than training a CNN from scratch.
crumbs: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project

title: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project
section: 8.5 The Questions This Chapter Teaches You to Ask
text:
8.5 The Questions This Chapter Teaches You to Ask
When you meet a new image task, start asking:

Do I have enough data to justify training from scratch?
If not, should I start from a pretrained model?
Should I train only the classification head first, or gradually unfreeze more layers later?

Once you start asking those questions, you are no longer just copying examples. You are making modeling decisions.
crumbs: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project

title: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project
section: 8.6 How You Could Extend This Template
text:
8.6 How You Could Extend This Template
To turn it into your own project, you would usually add:

your own dataset and image preprocessing
a train/validation split
training and validation loops
model checkpoint saving
gradual unfreezing if needed
crumbs: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project

title: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project
section: 8.7 The One Sentence I Want You to Keep
text:
8.7 The One Sentence I Want You to Keep
Transfer learning is not an “advanced trick.” It is a practical project strategy.
If your goal is to use PyTorch for your own needs, that strategic thinking matters more than memorizing one more network architecture.
crumbs: 8  Chapter 7 From a Transfer Learning Template to a Real Project

title: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up
text:
9.1 A Good General Rule
This chapter is not tied to one specific code file. Instead, it teaches you how to work with all the code that came before.
Because once you start building your own tasks, the first problems are usually not “I do not know how to write version one.”
They are more like:
So the goal of this chapter is simple:
to help you build a reliable way to debug your own projects.
When something goes wrong, do not change the model first.
Check these first:
Many problems are not caused by a weak model. They come from broken fundamentals.
crumbs: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up

title: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up
text:
Is the data correct?
Are the shapes correct?
Is the loss function appropriate?
Is the training loop correct?
crumbs: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up

title: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up
section: 9.2 If the Loss Does Not Go Down, Check in This Order
text:
9.2 If the Loss Does Not Go Down, Check in This Order

9.2.1 Step 1: Inspect one batch
Start by printing:
print(inputs.shape)
print(targets.shape)
print(targets[:5])
You want to verify:

the inputs are what you think they are
the labels are what you think they are
the batch shape is sensible

A surprising number of problems show up right here.


9.2.2 Step 2: Inspect the model output
outputs = model(inputs)
print(outputs.shape)
Now verify:

the output dimensions match the task
the output format matches what the loss function expects

For example, in a standard multiclass task:

outputs are often [batch_size, num_classes]
labels are often [batch_size]



9.2.3 Step 3: Make sure the loss function fits the task
This is easy to get wrong.
For example:

regression often uses MSELoss
binary classification often uses BCEWithLogitsLoss
multiclass classification often uses CrossEntropyLoss

If the task and the loss do not match, the model can behave very strangely.


9.2.4 Step 4: Verify the training loop itself
At minimum, check that these lines are present and ordered correctly:
optimizer.zero_grad()
loss.backward()
optimizer.step()


9.2.5 Step 5: Check whether the learning rate is extreme
If the learning rate is too large, the loss may oscillate or diverge.
If it is too small, the loss may barely move.
A very practical debugging move is to try a few different orders of magnitude:

1e-1
1e-2
1e-3
1e-4
crumbs: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up

title: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up
section: 9.3 How to Debug Shape Errors Quickly
text:
9.3 How to Debug Shape Errors Quickly
The fastest method is usually not staring at the error message. It is printing shapes at key points.
Especially these three:
print("inputs:", inputs.shape)
print("outputs:", outputs.shape)
print("targets:", targets.shape)
If you are working with a CNN, go one step further:
temporarily print intermediate shapes inside forward().
That is one of the fastest ways to find a dimension mismatch.
crumbs: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up

title: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up
section: 9.4 If Validation Performance Is Bad, What Should You Check?
text:
9.4 If Validation Performance Is Bad, What Should You Check?
If the code runs but results are poor, start with:

Are the training and validation sets properly separated?
Are you using model.train() during training?
Are you using model.eval() during validation?
Is the data preprocessing reasonable?
Does the metric actually match the goal of the task?

Do not stare only at the loss.
Sometimes loss goes down while the model is still learning the wrong thing.
crumbs: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up

title: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up
section: 9.5 Basic GPU and Performance Habits
text:
9.5 Basic GPU and Performance Habits
Once you start using a GPU, build at least these habits:

Put the model and data on the same device
Use torch.no_grad() during validation
If memory runs out, reduce batch size first
Make one batch work before scaling up

These are not fancy performance tricks. They are part of a stable development process.
crumbs: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up

title: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up
section: 9.6 Do Not Optimize Blindly When Training Is Slow
text:
9.6 Do Not Optimize Blindly When Training Is Slow
First ask where the bottleneck actually is:

data loading
model computation
batch size too small to use the GPU well
too much unnecessary printing

Optimization only helps once you know what you are optimizing.
crumbs: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up

title: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up
section: 9.7 A Very Practical Debugging Checklist
text:
9.7 A Very Practical Debugging Checklist
When you start your own task, run through this checklist:

Can I load one batch correctly?
Can I run one forward pass?
Can I run one backward pass?
Do output shape and label shape match?
Does loss show at least some downward trend within a few dozen steps?
Is validation performance better than random guessing?

If those six checks pass, your project is usually past the “completely broken” stage and ready for more meaningful iteration.
crumbs: 9  Chapter 8 Debugging and Speeding Things Up

title: 10  Chapter 9 Small Project Practice
text:
10.1 Project 1: Handwritten Digit Classifier
You do not really learn PyTorch by understanding examples. You learn it by building things yourself.
Here are three project directions that work well as practice.
Goal: build a full image classification task from scratch and get comfortable with data loading, training, validation, and model saving.
Good things to try:
crumbs: 10  Chapter 9 Small Project Practice

title: 10  Chapter 9 Small Project Practice
text:
change the width or depth of the model
compare an MLP with a CNN
record training curves
crumbs: 10  Chapter 9 Small Project Practice

title: 10  Chapter 9 Small Project Practice
section: 10.2 Project 2: Tabular Binary Classification
text:
10.2 Project 2: Tabular Binary Classification
Goal: use PyTorch on structured data rather than only on images.
Good things to try:

write a custom Dataset
process numerical features
train a binary classifier with BCEWithLogitsLoss
crumbs: 10  Chapter 9 Small Project Practice

title: 10  Chapter 9 Small Project Practice
section: 10.3 Project 3: Custom Image Classification
text:
10.3 Project 3: Custom Image Classification
Goal: use your own image folder to build a small classifier.
Good things to try:

organize your own data directory
add data augmentation
try transfer learning
crumbs: 10  Chapter 9 Small Project Practice

title: 10  Chapter 9 Small Project Practice
section: 10.4 Do Not Treat Projects Like Giant Assignments
text:
10.4 Do Not Treat Projects Like Giant Assignments
A better way to think about them is this:
you are reassembling the code skeletons you already studied.
For example:

the handwritten digit classifier is largely a remix of Chapters 5 and 6
tabular binary classification is a remix of Chapters 4 and 10
custom image classification is a remix of Chapters 7 and 10

That mindset makes projects feel much less intimidating.
You are not writing a system out of nowhere. You are recombining pieces you already understand.
crumbs: 10  Chapter 9 Small Project Practice

title: 10  Chapter 9 Small Project Practice
section: 10.5 Do Not Aim for Perfection in Version One
text:
10.5 Do Not Aim for Perfection in Version One
A better rhythm is:

make the smallest version run
add validation and logging
only then start tuning and swapping models

If you begin by trying to build a complete polished system all at once, you are much more likely to get stuck.
As you do these projects, keep practicing one important move:
translate the task into this small checklist:

What is the input?
What is the output?
What is the loss function?
What is the first model version?
What is the evaluation metric?

The more naturally you can answer those five questions, the closer you are to using PyTorch for your own needs.
crumbs: 10  Chapter 9 Small Project Practice

title: 10  Chapter 9 Small Project Practice
section: 10.6 Three Extra Skills Every Project Should Teach You
text:
10.6 Three Extra Skills Every Project Should Teach You

10.6.1 1. Record your experiments
At minimum, write down:

model version
learning rate
batch size
training result

Otherwise every new run starts to feel like starting from zero again.


10.6.2 2. Save your models
Even in a first project, practice saving:

the final epoch model
or the best model on the validation set



10.6.3 3. Write a minimal prediction script
A project is not complete just because you can train.
You also want to be able to load a trained model and make predictions with it.
Even a very small prediction script builds the right habit.
crumbs: 10  Chapter 9 Small Project Practice

title: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch
text:
11.1 Start with the General Template
If this book only leaves you able to understand the examples inside it, that is not enough.
The real goal is this:
when you face your own task later, you should be able to translate it into a PyTorch project.
This chapter is here to train exactly that skill.
Open code/08_project_template.py.
The code is intentionally plain. It is not trying to show clever tricks. It is meant to give you a starting point that you can actually reshape for your own work.
It contains four parts that you will almost always need to modify:
crumbs: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch

title: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch
text:
YourDataset
YourModel
criterion
train_one_epoch()
crumbs: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch

title: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch
section: 11.2 What Does Each Part of the Template Do?
text:
11.2 What Does Each Part of the Template Do?

11.2.1 YourDataset
This defines:

what your raw data looks like
what one sample should return

In other words, it solves the data entry problem.


11.2.2 YourModel
This defines:

which layers the input passes through
what format the final output has

In other words, it solves the model structure problem.


11.2.3 criterion
This defines:

what standard you use to judge whether the current prediction is good or bad

In other words, it defines the optimization target.


11.2.4 train_one_epoch()
This defines:

how parameters get updated repeatedly during one epoch of training

In other words, it defines the training process.
When you begin building your own task, most of the work is some variation of editing those four parts.
crumbs: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch

title: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch
section: 11.3 Every Task Should Start with These Four Questions
text:
11.3 Every Task Should Start with These Four Questions

11.3.1 1. What is your input?
Is it images, text, tabular data, or a time series?
That decision affects:

how to write the Dataset
what the input tensor shape should be
what preprocessing is needed



11.3.2 2. What is your output?
Is it a continuous value, one category, or multiple labels?
That decision affects:

the design of the final output layer
the choice of loss function



11.3.3 3. How do you define success?
This determines which metrics matter:

regression often uses error-based metrics
classification often uses accuracy, recall, or similar metrics
ranking and generation tasks use different metrics again



11.3.4 4. What training skeleton will you use?
This affects:

how batching works
which optimizer you choose
whether you need a validation set
whether you should save the best model
crumbs: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch

title: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch
section: 11.4 Turn Those Questions into Concrete Decisions
text:
11.4 Turn Those Questions into Concrete Decisions
Many people still get stuck here. They know they should think about inputs and outputs, but they do not know how to turn that into code.
So here is a more direct way to think about it.

11.4.1 If your output is a single number
It is probably a regression task.
That usually suggests:

output dimension of 1
floating-point labels
a loss like MSELoss



11.4.2 If your output is one of two classes
It is probably a binary classification task.
That usually suggests:

output dimension of 1
a loss like BCEWithLogitsLoss
turning the output into a probability or class prediction afterward



11.4.3 If your output is one of many classes
It is probably a multiclass classification task.
That usually suggests:

an output dimension equal to the number of classes
a loss like CrossEntropyLoss
labels stored as class indices
crumbs: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch

title: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch
section: 11.5 How Might You Modify the Template?
text:
11.5 How Might You Modify the Template?

11.5.1 For tabular binary classification
You would often change:

YourDataset: load tabular features and labels
YourModel: make the final output 1-dimensional
criterion: switch to BCEWithLogitsLoss



11.5.2 For multiclass image classification
You would often change:

YourDataset: use an image dataset
YourModel: use a CNN or a pretrained model
criterion: keep CrossEntropyLoss



11.5.3 For regression
You would often change:

the final output dimension to 1
the labels to floating-point values
the loss to MSELoss
crumbs: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch

title: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch
section: 11.6 One Step Further: How Do You Go from a Task Description to Code?
text:
11.6 One Step Further: How Do You Go from a Task Description to Code?
Suppose your future task sounds like one of these:

“I want to predict whether a user will churn from tabular features.”
“I want to sort my own fruit photos into four classes.”
“I want to predict tomorrow’s sales from historical values.”

Force yourself to write these five sentences first:

What shape is my input?
What type of output do I need?
How should I prepare the labels?
What is the simplest sensible first model?
What are my loss function and metric?

If you can answer those five questions clearly, your code already has a direction.
crumbs: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch

title: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch
section: 11.7 What You Really Want to Carry Forward
text:
11.7 What You Really Want to Carry Forward
Not one exact code snippet, but a decision framework:

identify the task type
define the input and output format
match the model and loss function to the task
plug everything into a training template

Once that framework becomes natural, new tasks stop feeling like they require starting from zero.
crumbs: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch

title: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch
section: 11.8 An Important Exercise
text:
11.8 An Important Exercise
Pick one small task you genuinely care about and write only these four lines:

What is my input?
What is my output?
What loss function will I probably use?
What should the first version of my model be?

If you can do that on your own, you have already started using PyTorch for your own needs.
crumbs: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch

title: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch
section: 11.9 This Chapter Is Really the Destination of the Whole Book
text:
11.9 This Chapter Is Really the Destination of the Whole Book
Everything earlier in the book points here:

tensors and shapes help you describe inputs and outputs
autograd and training loops help you understand how learning happens
Dataset and DataLoader help you organize your own data
MLPs, CNNs, and transfer learning give you several practical modeling starting points

So by the end, the most important thing is not “how many APIs did I memorize?”
It is this:
“When I get a new problem, I know how to begin the first version of a PyTorch project.”
crumbs: 11  Chapter 10 Translating Your Own Problem into PyTorch

title: 12  Summary
text:
The core idea of this version of the book is code first, from the ground up.
That means we do not start by piling up abstract concepts and then adding a few examples at the end. Instead, we start with a chain of runnable code and pull out the ideas from there: tensors, gradients, training, data flow, and model design.
If you work through the code/ directory in order, you build a stable mental path:

understand tensors and shapes
understand gradients and backpropagation
understand the training loop
understand how data becomes batches
understand how classification models come together
understand how CNNs and transfer learning expand what you can build

Once you can look at a training script and quickly locate the data, model, loss, optimizer, and validation logic, PyTorch stops feeling like scattered knowledge.
More importantly, this book is trying to build transfer ability:
when a new task appears in front of you, you should be able to break it into inputs, outputs, a dataset, a model, a loss function, and a training process, instead of hunting for a tutorial that matches it perfectly.
Once you can do that, PyTorch has started to become your tool rather than just something you studied.
crumbs: 12  Summary
